content.content:
150	---
151	
152	## 10. Model Selection Criteria
153	
154	Primary embedding model selection follows the two-phase protocol from ADR-0013. The selection criterion is fixed here and is not changed after any SCF data is examined.
155	
156	**Candidate model set diversity requirement:**
157	The candidate set must include at minimum: one or more general-purpose models (all-MiniLM, BGE, E5 family) plus two diversity anchors — (1) a model domain fine-tuned on legal/regulatory text; (2) a model from a different architecture class (sparse retrieval or substantially different training corpus). T-01 identifies specific models during Phase 0. Diversity anchor selection logged to Preregistration Ledger before Phase 1 runs. Phase 1 does not run without both anchors present.
158	
159	**Phase 1 — Benchmark evaluation (no SCF data):**
160	Candidate models evaluated on: (a) STS-B and SICK-R (general-purpose, coarse filter only — see Section 12 for domain shift limitation); (b) any regulatory/legal domain STS benchmarks identified by T-01 during Phase 0 — if found, added; if not found, absence documented and Phase 2 weighted more heavily; (c) SCF structural metadata anchor pairs: similarity anchors = STRM '= Equal To' pairs; dissimilarity anchors = STRM '∅ No Relation' pairs across unrelated domains. Selection criterion: composite of Spearman correlation on benchmark sets and anchor consistency score. Produces ranked shortlist of 3–5 models.
161	
162	**Phase 2 — Calibration sample evaluation (validation split):**
163	Shortlisted models evaluated on the validation split (15% of SCF controls) using:
164	- Silhouette score on the SCF control embedding space
165	- Anchor consistency score: proportion of '= Equal To' anchor pairs ranked above '∅ No Relation' anchor pairs
166	- Cross-model agreement: pairwise Spearman between models' pairwise similarity rankings across the full candidate set including diversity anchors
167	
168	**Primary model**: Model with highest composite score across all three Phase 2 criteria. Designation logged to Preregistration Ledger with cryptographic timestamp before Gate 2 split executes.
169	
170	**All other models**: Designated as secondary/sensitivity analyses at the same timestamp. Model selection is not revisited after Gate 2.
171	
172	**OPEN DECISION — DIVERGENCE-01:** Operationalization of model-STRM divergence metric. Must be selected and logged to the Preregistration Ledger before any STRM comparison results are examined. Candidate approaches: (A) Map STRM categories to ordinal numeric scale + Spearman rank correlation; (B) Threshold model similarity scores into STRM categories + agreement rate; (C) Polychoric correlation treating STRM as ordinal; (D) Rank preservation test. Selection criterion: choose the operationalization that makes fewest assumptions about STRM category spacing given the observed label distribution from EDA. This is a hard Gate 2 prerequisite — STRM comparison results cannot be examined before DIVERGENCE-01 is closed.
173	
174	---
175	
176	## 11. Reporting Standards
177	
178	- Effect size and confidence interval reported for every primary and secondary result
179	- Confidence intervals: 95%, bootstrap (10,000 resamples) unless parametric assumptions are verified
180	- Null results reported with effect size estimate and CI — "no significant difference" without a CI is not a finding
181	- All figures include uncertainty visualization (error bars, CI ribbons, or equivalent)
182	- Subgroup analyses pre-specified in Section 2; post-hoc subgroup analyses labeled exploratory
183	
184	---
185	
186	## 12. Limitations and Design Tradeoffs
187	
188	This section is required. It acknowledges known limitations of the design before any results are seen. It is written here so it cannot be omitted or softened after results are known.
189	
190	### Exploratory-Guided Hypothesis Selection
191	
192	The SAP is finalized at Gate 2, after exploratory analysis. This is intentional — specifying effect sizes before seeing the data produces authorial guesses presented as pre-specified thresholds (see issue #59 resolution). But this design has a cost: hypotheses entering Gate 2 are selected based on what looked tractable or promising in the exploratory phase. This is not traditional pre-registration. It is a two-stage adaptive design with a registration checkpoint between stages.
193	
194	**Nature of the bias:** Effect sizes for data-adaptive hypotheses are subject to inflation relative to theory-derived hypotheses. Hypotheses that looked uninteresting in exploratory analysis are less likely to be registered, even if they would have been pre-specified before data was seen.
195	
196	**Mitigation 1 — Multiple testing correction:** Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction applied across all confirmatory hypotheses regardless of whether they are theory-derived or data-adaptive. Correction is applied to the full registered hypothesis set, not a subset selected after results.
197	
198	**Mitigation 2 — Mandatory null result reporting:** Every hypothesis that enters the confirmatory phase is reported in all outputs regardless of outcome. Selective reporting of significant findings is not permitted. The full hypothesis set and all test results appear in the Preregistration Ledger and in any published appendix.
199	
200	**Mitigation 3 — Epistemic status labeling:** All findings are labeled with their epistemic origin — `theory-derived` (pre-specified from literature/theory before any SCF data was seen), `data-adaptive` (registered before confirmatory test but generated from exploratory findings), or `exploratory` (not pre-registered). Labels appear in all outputs including publications. Readers assess inference strength for each finding independently.
201	
202	This limitation is disclosed in the methods section of all publications. The adaptive design is not a flaw to hide — it is the appropriate design for an autonomous iterative research system operating on data it has not yet seen. The mitigations above provide the transparency that makes adaptive findings interpretable.
203	
204	### Phase 1 Benchmark Domain Shift
205	
206	STS-B and SICK-R were built from everyday language corpora (image captions, news headlines). They do not test GRC-domain semantic distinctions. Phase 1 benchmark results are treated as a coarse filter only. Domain-appropriate evaluation relies on SCF structural metadata anchor pairs and Phase 2 calibration sample results. A human-labeled SCF control pair gold standard evaluation set does not currently exist and is designated as a post-publication community contribution milestone.
207	
208	### Anchor Consistency Circularity
209	
210	The anchor consistency score uses STRM-asserted relationship pairs ('= Equal To', '∅ No Relation') as weak supervision. STRM reliability is simultaneously under investigation as part of the secondary analysis. This creates a partial circularity: STRM assertions are used to validate the embedding while their reliability is being tested. The circularity is partial — silhouette score and cross-model agreement are independent of STRM — and is disclosed here prior to any results being examined.
211	
212	### STRM Inter-Rater Reliability
213	
214	STRM inter-rater reliability (Fleiss kappa on a re-rated sample) requires GRC domain expert raters. Two paths are available: (1) rater recruitment during Phase 0 — if selected, documented as a Gate 1 prerequisite; (2) if rater recruitment is infeasible, STRM reliability is unestimated. In path 2, the interpretation requiring evidence of STRM reliability is unavailable and model-STRM divergence cannot be attributed to annotator inconsistency vs. systematic annotation error. Path selection is logged to the Preregistration Ledger before Gate 1. Rater recruitment is also designated as a post-publication community contribution milestone.
215

content.text:
Now I have enough context. Let me compile my findings. The core issue is that while the methodology documents extensively describe a train/val/test split with a strict firewall between exploratory and confirmatory phases, they do not adequately address a fundamental methodological problem: this split strategy is scientifically valid for some types of analyses (P1, correlations with individual mappings) but is fundamentally incompatible with other types of analyses (P2 graph topology, P3/P4 coverage properties, P5 clustering across the whole framework).

Let me create a comprehensive analysis:

input.lens: Methodology and Architecture Sufficiency for Achieving the Research Goal

findings.title: Train/Val/Test Split Invalidates Graph Topology Confirmatory Claims (P2)
findings.problem: P2's research questions ask about the structure of the ENTIRE control graph: 'do the 33 editorial domains correspond to natural semantic communities?' and 'is graph density uniform across domains?' These are properties of the whole graph, not sample-based statistics. A random 70/15/15 train/val/test split of control nodes fundamentally breaks the graph topology by removing edges and isolated nodes. Community detection algorithms (Leiden, Louvain) on a 70% subgraph will produce different community structures than on the 100% graph because: (a) removing nodes removes their edges, changing centrality scores; (b) modularity Q changes based on which nodes are present; (c) the hypothesis 'community structure differs from editorial domains' is a property of the complete graph, not a random sample. Running community detection on train+val (70+15=85%) in exploratory phase and re-running on test (15%) in confirmatory phase will produce incompatible community structures. The test-only confirmatory analysis cannot logically confirm/reject a hypothesis about whole-graph topology when it analyzes only 15% of the nodes.
findings.evidence: PROJECT_CHARTER.md H2.1: 'Community detection on the STRM-weighted control graph produces communities that differ significantly from the 33 editorial domains' — this hypothesis is about THE control graph, not a sample. Methods Scaffolding §P2: 'Graph topology metrics: Degree distribution, betweenness centrality, eigenvector centrality, clustering coefficient. Computed on the directed STRM graph.' — 'the graph' is implicitly the whole graph. The code (p2_control_topology/agent.py) has no documented strategy for handling split-based graph partitioning. The state machine (graph.py) loads splits but does not document how graph-based confirmatory tests adapt to a subset of nodes.
findings.recommendation: Either: (A) Reframe P2 hypotheses to explicitly be about sample-based graph properties (degree distribution on control sample, not graph structure), which requires rewriting H2.1–H2.5 to be testable on subgraphs; (B) Exempt P2 from the exploratory/confirmatory split and conduct all P2 analysis on the full train+val corpus (85%), reporting it as exploratory (descriptive) rather than confirmatory; (C) Adopt a graph-split strategy (e.g., edge-based stratification) that preserves community structure across splits, but this requires substantial methodological rework and is not currently documented. The current design is internally inconsistent — either the split or the hypothesis set must change.

findings.title: Coverage Properties Cannot Be Tested on Held-Out Subsets (P3, P4)
findings.problem: P3 (Regulatory Convergence Atlas) and P4 (Risk Blindspot Engine) answer questions about COVERAGE and DISTRIBUTION properties of the entire STRM mapping corpus: 'which domains have multi-jurisdictional consensus?' (P3 H3.2: 'A small subset of domains accounts for >60% of all mappings'); 'which of the 39 risk categories are systematically underserved?' (P4 H4.1: 'Coverage is non-uniform'). These are properties of the WHOLE CORPUS, not statistics that generalize from a sample. If you remove 15% of controls at random, you remove some of their mappings, changing the distribution of mappings across domains, changing coverage percentages for risk categories. A risk category with 30% coverage in the full corpus may have 28% in a 85% subsample and 35% in the 15% test subsample — these numbers are inconsistent. You cannot confirm H4.1 ('coverage is non-uniform') using a test set that has a different coverage distribution than the training set due to random sampling. The confirmatory claim would be: 'in this 15% subsample, coverage is non-uniform,' not 'in the SCF corpus, coverage is non-uniform.' The hypothesis tested on the test set would be about a different population than the hypothesis formed in exploratory analysis.
findings.evidence: PROJECT_CHARTER.md H3.2: 'A small subset of SCF domains (< 20%) accounts for > 60% of all STRM mappings' — this is about the distribution IN THE CORPUS. H4.1: 'Framework portfolio coverage of the 39 risk categories is non-uniform' — coverage is a corpus-level property. Methods Scaffolding §P4: 'Coverage distribution analysis: Distribution of control counts per risk category. Summary statistics: mean, median, Gini coefficient (coverage inequality).' — Gini coefficient on the full corpus differs from Gini on a 15% sample. The agent code (p4_risk_blindspot/agent.py) provides no documentation of how to handle test-set-only coverage analysis that is structurally different from train+val coverage.
findings.recommendation: Either: (A) Reframe P3 and P4 hypotheses as 'comparative' rather than absolute — test whether the rank-order of coverage across domains is preserved between train+val and test splits, rather than testing absolute coverage values; (B) Conduct P3 and P4 on the full train+val corpus (85%) and label all findings as exploratory, not confirmatory; (C) If confirmatory P3/P4 analysis is necessary, adopt a stratified-split strategy that preserves the domain×mapping distribution, accepting that this may produce unequal sample sizes per domain and requires power analysis per stratum. The current design conflates 'testing on a held-out set' with 'confirmatory testing' without accounting for when held-out-set tests produce incompatible null hypotheses.

findings.title: P5 Clustering Confirmatory Analysis Requires Full-Corpus Semantic Space (Boundary Condition Ambiguity)
findings.problem: P5 aims to identify controls 'semantically entangled with AI governance' via clustering. The research question is: 'which controls across all 33 SCF domains are entangled' — 'all 33 domains' suggests the full control corpus. The confirmatory hypothesis H5.1 is: 'A significant portion (>25%) of AI governance-relevant controls fall outside the AAT domain' — this is asking about the composition of the ENTIRE AI governance cluster relative to the AAT domain boundary. If you remove 15% of controls at random before clustering, the cluster composition changes: controls that would cluster with AI governance in the full corpus may fail to do so in an 85% subset if their nearest neighbors are in the held-out 15%. Conversely, controls in the test set that should not cluster with AI governance might do so if key reference controls are missing. The boundary of what 'is entangled with AI governance' depends on the full embedding space. Running K-means or HDBSCAN with fixed hyperparameters (min_cluster_size, min_samples) on two different control subsets will produce different cluster assignments for the same controls, making the confirmatory test incomparable to the exploratory analysis.
findings.evidence: PROJECT_CHARTER.md H5.1: 'A significant portion (> 25%) of AI governance-relevant SCF controls fall outside the AAT domain when identified via NLP clustering' — the 25% threshold and the cluster membership are both dependent on which controls are available for clustering. Methods Scaffolding §P5: 'min_cluster_size and min_samples are explored in train split, pre-specified at SAP lock, not re-tuned after' — the hyperparameters are optimized on 70% of the data and then applied to a different subset (test) in confirmatory phase. Clustering algorithms are sensitive to data composition at the boundary (points near cluster edges flip membership based on the full dataset density). No document addresses whether the clustering boundary is stable across subsamples.
findings.recommendation: Document an explicit assumption in the SAP: 'P5 clustering confirmatory analysis assumes that HDBSCAN cluster membership is sufficiently stable across 85% vs. 100% of the control corpus (Procrustes alignment of cluster assignments >85% match) OR revise P5 to be fully exploratory and report clustering findings as hypothesis-generating for future work. Also: conduct a sensitivity analysis in exploratory phase showing that core AI governance cluster membership (top 50 controls by cosine similarity to AI governance LRFs) is consistent between train+val and full corpus. If not, the confirmatory design is invalid.

findings.title: Firewall Data Leak Risk: Exploratory Effect Sizes Calibrate Confirmatory Test Power Without Structural Enforcement
findings.problem: The design document (ADR-0007) acknowledges that 'exploratory findings are calibrated to the actual data before hypotheses are committed' to avoid 'poorly-calibrated' pre-specifications. The SAP (§12) explicitly documents 'Effect sizes for data-adaptive hypotheses are subject to inflation relative to theory-derived hypotheses.' The mitigation listed is 'Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction applied across all confirmatory hypotheses' — but FDR correction does not fix effect size inflation; it only controls false discovery rate. The structural firewall (test split physically inaccessible before Gate 2) is correctly implemented in LangGraph. However, the SAP documents no enforcement mechanism preventing calibration bias: exploratory analysis on train+val can produce 'promising' effect sizes that attract hypothesis registration, while the same hypotheses might have smaller true effects in the test set due to sampling variation or regression to the mean. The design acknowledges the bias exists (§12) but relies on reporting transparency ('epistemic status labeling') to mitigate it, not structural prevention. This is an audit-trail mitigation, not a methodology mitigation.
findings.evidence: ADR-0007: 'Exploratory findings are calibrated to the actual data before hypotheses are committed.' SAP §12: 'Effect sizes for data-adaptive hypotheses are subject to inflation relative to theory-derived hypotheses.' Methods Scaffolding §2: 'Exploratory findings are reported with descriptive statistics, visualizations, and directional language. No p-values, no significance claims.' — but the effect sizes from exploratory analysis DIRECTLY INFORM which hypotheses are registered for confirmatory testing. The correlation between exploratory effect size and probability of registration is not documented or controlled. A weak-effect hypothesis (ρ=0.1, p=0.08 in exploratory) is less likely to be registered than a strong-effect hypothesis (ρ=0.4, p=0.01 in exploratory), biasing the confirmatory hypothesis set toward inflated effects.
findings.recommendation: Formalize the effect-size selection process: (1) At Gate 2, list all effect sizes observed in exploratory analysis and document which ones motivated hypothesis registration; (2) Require effect size pre-specifications to be explicitly decoupled from exploratory observations — e.g., 'exploratory finding was ρ=0.3, but pre-specified effect size for confirmatory test is ρ≥0.2 (conservative)' or 'ρ≥0.5 (stricter than exploratory)'; (3) If pre-specified effect size differs from exploratory, document the reasoning; (4) Use conditional power analysis on the pre-specified effect size to determine whether the test set is large enough. Current design allows the exploratory effect size to 'leak' into hypothesis strength thresholds without explicit acknowledgment.

findings.title: Unit of Analysis Declared (Control ID) but Not Enforced Across Analyses (P1 vs. P2–P5 Mismatch)
findings.problem: The SAP (§7) specifies: 'Unit of analysis: SCF control (~1,400 unique controls). Not mapping rows (280,000).' The data_split.py code enforces this: 'Split strategy: shuffle-and-slice on UNIQUE SCF control IDs... Passing mapping rows (280,000) instead of control IDs (1,400) would produce leakage.' This is correct for P1 (correlation between NLP similarity and STRM strength scores at the mapping level, but grouped by control for independence). However, P2–P5 hypotheses are phrased in terms of different units: P2 is about CONTROLS as graph nodes; P3 is about MAPPINGS (to quantify convergence); P4 is about RISK CATEGORIES (to measure coverage); P5 is about CONTROLS as clustering members. The analysis modules (p1_strm_nlp, p2_control_topology, etc.) do not document which unit of analysis they adopt, and the agent code is unimplemented (NotImplementedError). The risk: when analysis is implemented, P3 or P4 might load the full 280,000 mapping rows for coverage analysis, not grouped by control, invalidating the train/val/test split assumption (different rows of the same control can appear in different splits).
findings.evidence: Data split.py: 'CALLER CONTRACT: control_ids must be deduplicated before calling — one ID per control, not one row per STRM mapping.' SAP §7: 'Unit of analysis: SCF control (~1,400 unique controls).' P3 agent code (p3_regulatory_convergence/agent.py) specifies inputs as 'STRM equivalence mappings: data/splits/ (relationship_type == "equal")' — mapping-level granularity. P4 agent specifies: 'Control-to-risk mappings: ara/artifacts/ (SCF native risk assignments)' — also mapping-level. The split ensures control-level leakage does not occur; it says nothing about mapping-level leakage if agents load individual mapping rows (e.g., train_mappings = all rows where control_id in train_ids, which exposes mapping-level sampling variation across splits).
findings.recommendation: Require each analysis module to explicitly document in its code header: 'Unit of analysis: [CONTROL | MAPPING | RISK_CATEGORY]' and include a pre-execution assertion that validates the split strategy is compatible with that unit. If P3 or P4 analyses are mapping-level (which makes sense for coverage), document that many rows per control are present in each split and power analysis must account for intra-control correlation (mixed-effects model or effective sample size adjustment). If control-level analysis is required, ensure mapping rows are aggregated (mean, max, median) before analysis, not disaggregated.

findings.title: Gate 2 SAP Finalization Accepts Unresolved Methodological Tensions
findings.problem: Gate 2 is documented as the 'point of no return for the exploratory/confirmatory firewall' — when test split is released and confirmatory analysis begins. The gate coordinator checks that hypotheses are registered and SAP is locked. However, there is no documented pre-Gate-2 validation that the SAP is internally consistent with the split strategy. No checklist verifies: (1) For each hypothesis, is the unit of analysis compatible with the split? (2) For graph/coverage/clustering hypotheses, are they redefined to be compatible with subgraph/subsample analysis? (3) Are effect sizes pre-specified, not lifted from exploratory analysis? (4) Is power analysis complete for the test set size (15%)? A poorly-written SAP (e.g., one that retains graph-topology hypotheses unchanged from the charter) can pass Gate 2 without triggering the firewall violations because Gate 2 checks registration status, not internal consistency. The gate is a structural enforcement point for data access (test split release), not a methodological validation point.
findings.evidence: ADR-0007: 'Gate 2 is the point of no return... the test split is physically inaccessible before that gate passes.' This is implemented in src/graph.py and enforced by the LangGraph topology. But the gate node function (gates.py, gate_2_node) does not perform SAP validation. The SAP template (§1–7) has fields for 'Primary Analysis,' 'Secondary Analyses,' 'Sensitivity Analyses,' 'Hypothesis Register,' but no field for 'Confirmatory Analysis Compatibility with Data Split.' A SAP could list all 30+ hypotheses from the charter and still pass Gate 2 if the gate only checks that the set is non-empty and registered.
findings.recommendation: Add a pre-Gate-2 validation checklist (documented in GATES.md or the gate_2_node code): For each confirmed hypothesis, verify: (a) Unit of analysis matches split strategy; (b) For graph/coverage hypotheses, confirm they are redefined for subgraph/subsample or exempted; (c) Effect sizes are pre-specified and justified (exploratory or conservative); (d) Power analysis completed on test set size; (e) Multiple comparisons correction family defined. Gate 2 must confirm these items before releasing the test split. This is not a change to the gate structure, just a clarification of what 'SAP locked' means.

findings.title: NIST Cluster Independence Constraint Underspecified for P3/P4/P5
findings.where: docs/protocol/04_methods_scaffolding.md §P1 (NIST constraint clearly stated); src/agents/p3_regulatory_convergence/agent.py (constraint mentioned but not operationalized); src/agents/p4_risk_blindspot/agent.py (constraint not mentioned)
findings.problem: The NIST cluster constraint ('NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 are one authorship cluster and must not be treated as independent evidence') is explicitly documented for P1 in Methods Scaffolding: 'NIST cluster independence constraint: NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 mappings are treated as ONE evidence source.' The same constraint is stated for P3: 'within-NIST-cluster convergence is not treated as independent evidence of cross-framework convergence.' But P2 and P4 agent code do not mention the constraint at all. For P2 (graph topology), if the STRM mappings include edges from all three NIST frameworks and the graph is not split by framework origin, community detection may find a 'NIST cluster community' (all NIST controls clustering together) and report it as evidence of independent convergence — which violates the constraint. For P4 (coverage), if a risk category is well-covered by all three NIST frameworks, the agent might count it as 'triple coverage' when it should be counted as 'NIST-cluster coverage' (one evidence source). The constraint is clear for P1 but operationalization is delegated to individual agents without specification.
findings.evidence: Methods Scaffolding §P1: 'NIST cluster independence constraint: NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 mappings are treated as ONE evidence source... This constraint is enforced at the data layer, not at the analysis layer — it is not a choice made per analysis, it is a standing rule.' But p3_regulatory_convergence/agent.py specifies: 'NIST cluster constraint (MANDATORY): NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 are ONE authorship cluster' with no implementation details. The P2 and P4 agent stubs have no mention of the constraint. Data layer enforcement would mean filtering the split data to remove NIST intra-cluster edges before loading — but data_split.py makes no such distinction (it splits on control IDs, not mapping origin).
findings.recommendation: Explicitly operationalize the constraint: (1) In data_split.py or the DataStewardAgent, add an optional filter that tags each STRM mapping with its origin framework (NIST vs. non-NIST). (2) In each agent's code (P2–P5), document the constraint and implement it: P2 should tag community assignments with framework origin and report cross-framework vs. NIST-only clusters separately; P3 should exclude NIST intra-cluster dyads from convergence statistics; P4 should weight NIST-framework coverage as 0.33x (one framework, three implementations) not 3.0x; P5 should handle NIST control clustering separately. (3) Include a pre-execution assertion in each module: 'NIST cluster constraint applied: [COUNT] NIST intra-cluster findings flagged and excluded.' If the operationalization is deferred to individual agents without a shared implementation, the constraint will be inconsistently applied.

findings.title: LangGraph Architecture Supports Exploratory/Confirmatory Firewall but Not Methodology Validation
findings.problem: The LangGraph architecture correctly implements the structural firewall: test split is encrypted, Gate 2 releases the key, confirmatory analysis runs on test-only data. This is sound from a data-access-control perspective. However, the architecture does not validate that the methodology is sound for the questions being asked. The gate nodes check that hypotheses are registered (state.hypothesis_set is non-empty) but do not validate that hypotheses are appropriate for the split strategy. An agent could register a hypothesis like 'Community structure of the control graph differs from SCF domains' (which is invalid for test-only analysis) and the gate would pass it. The structural firewall prevents running confirmatory analysis on train+val data, but it does not prevent running invalid confirmatory analysis on test data. This is a gap between 'structural enforcement of data access' and 'methodological enforcement of valid analysis.'
findings.evidence: src/graph.py: gate_2 routes to either 'confirmatory_entry' or back to 'exploratory_phase' based on a gate decision, but the decision is recorded in state.gate_status[gate_2] with fields: 'gate_id', 'decision', 'rationale', 'reviewer'. The 'rationale' field is a string (reviewed by a human), not a structured checklist of methodological validations. src/nodes/gates.py: gate_2_node has no documented pre-check of hypothesis compatibility. The gate is a 'human approval gate' (ADR-0004 §2) but relies on the human reviewer to check methodology — structural validation is not in place.
findings.recommendation: Add a pre-Gate-2 validation function (gate_2_validation.py) that: (1) For each registered hypothesis, identifies the analysis type (correlation/classification vs. graph property vs. coverage); (2) Checks the split strategy compatibility; (3) Flags graph/coverage hypotheses as requiring exemption or redefinition; (4) Reports a structured validation report to the gate coordinator. The human reviewer can then make an informed decision ('Approve SAP' vs. 'Reject SAP and require revision'). This does not reduce human judgment; it surfaces the methodological issues for the human to decide on.

findings.title: Tier 2 Knowledge Graph Composition Assumes P2 Topology is Composable but Split Strategy May Prevent This
findings.problem: ADR-0010 specifies: 'P2 topology graphs must be designed for multi-graph composition. Tier 2 will ingest multiple framework graphs and run cross-graph algorithms.' The PROJECT_CHARTER requires: 'P2 topology graphs must be designed for multi-graph composition.' But if P2 confirmatory analysis is conducted on 15% of controls (test split), the P2 knowledge graph will represent only 15% of the SCF control space. When Tier 2 tries to compose multiple such subgraph outputs (P2 from SCF alone, P2 from NIST 800-53, P2 from CIS v8), it will be composing incomplete, non-representative topology graphs. Tier 2's cross-framework 'blast radius modeling' (tracing failures through the control graph) will lose edges that exist in the full corpus, producing underestimates of propagation. The program goal is 'knowledge graphs of each framework' and 'blast radius modeling'; if the graphs are subsampled, the modeling is invalid. This is a direct threat to the Tier 2 research objective.
findings.evidence: ADR-0010: 'Graph data models must compose... Tier 2 will ingest multiple framework graphs and run cross-graph algorithms... information cascade modeling on the combined graph.' PROJECT_CHARTER §1.5: 'P2 topology graphs must be designed for multi-graph composition.' But if P2 uses a 70/15/15 split and only confirmatory results (test set) are exported as knowledge graphs, those graphs have 15% of the nodes. Tier 2 cannot perform 'blast radius modeling' on an incomplete graph. Conversely, if all P2 exploratory results (85% of controls) are exported, they are not confirmatory and Tier 2 is building impact models on exploratory findings.
findings.recommendation: At Gate 2, decide: (A) Export P2 knowledge graphs from the full train+val corpus (85%), label all Tier 2 inputs as exploratory, and conduct Tier 2 analysis with that epistemic status; (B) Redesign P2 to produce confirmatory results on the full corpus and label all P2 findings as confirmatory; (C) Restructure Tier 1 so that P2 is a separate 'topology project' with its own full-corpus data access, independent of P1/P3/P4/P5 module sequencing. Option A is the most practical given current architecture. Option B requires the methodological fix noted in the P2 finding (redefine hypotheses for subgraph analysis or exempt from split). Option C requires an architectural change (separate graphs for topology vs. mapping analysis).

findings.title: Train/Val/Test Split Invalidates Graph Topology Confirmatory Claims (P2)
findings.problem: P2's research questions ask about the structure of the ENTIRE control graph: 'do the 33 editorial domains correspond to natural semantic communities?' and 'is graph density uniform across domains?' These are properties of the whole graph, not sample-based statistics. A random 70/15/15 train/val/test split of control nodes fundamentally breaks the graph topology by removing edges and isolated nodes. Community detection algorithms (Leiden, Louvain) on a 70% subgraph will produce different community structures than on the 100% graph because: (a) removing nodes removes their edges, changing centrality scores; (b) modularity Q changes based on which nodes are present; (c) the hypothesis 'community structure differs from editorial domains' is a property of the complete graph, not a random sample. Running community detection on train+val (70+15=85%) in exploratory phase and re-running on test (15%) in confirmatory phase will produce incompatible community structures. The test-only confirmatory analysis cannot logically confirm/reject a hypothesis about whole-graph topology when it analyzes only 15% of the nodes.
findings.evidence: PROJECT_CHARTER.md H2.1: 'Community detection on the STRM-weighted control graph produces communities that differ significantly from the 33 editorial domains' — this hypothesis is about THE control graph, not a sample. Methods Scaffolding §P2: 'Graph topology metrics: Degree distribution, betweenness centrality, eigenvector centrality, clustering coefficient. Computed on the directed STRM graph.' — 'the graph' is implicitly the whole graph. The code (p2_control_topology/agent.py) has no documented strategy for handling split-based graph partitioning. The state machine (graph.py) loads splits but does not document how graph-based confirmatory tests adapt to a subset of nodes.

(2) For graph/coverage/clustering hypotheses, are they redefined to be compatible with subgraph/subsample analysis? (3) Are effect sizes pre-specified, not lifted from exploratory analysis? (4) Is power analysis complete for the test set size (15%)? A poorly-written SAP (e.g., one that retains graph-topology hypotheses unchanged from the charter) can pass Gate 2 without triggering the firewall violations because Gate 2 checks registration status, not internal consistency. The gate is a structural enforcement point for data access (test split release), not a methodological validation point.
findings.evidence: ADR-0007: 'Gate 2 is the point of no return... the test split is physically inaccessible before that gate passes.' This is implemented in src/graph.py and enforced by the LangGraph topology. But the gate node function (gates.py, gate_2_node) does not perform SAP validation. The SAP template (§1–7) has fields for 'Primary Analysis,' 'Secondary Analyses,' 'Sensitivity Analyses,' 'Hypothesis Register,' but no field for 'Confirmatory Analysis Compatibility with Data Split.' A SAP could list all 30+ hypotheses from the charter and still pass Gate 2 if the gate only checks that the set is non-empty and registered.
findings.recommendation: Add a pre-Gate-2 validation checklist (documented in GATES.md or the gate_2_node code): For each confirmed hypothesis, verify: (a) Unit of analysis matches split strategy; (b) For graph/coverage hypotheses, confirm they are redefined for subgraph/subsample or exempted; (c) Effect sizes are pre-specified and justified (exploratory or conservative); (d) Power analysis completed on test set size; (e) Multiple comparisons correction family defined. Gate 2 must confirm these items before releasing the test split. This is not a change to the gate structure, just a clarification of what 'SAP locked' means.

findings.title: NIST Cluster Independence Constraint Underspecified for P3/P4/P5
findings.where: docs/protocol/04_methods_scaffolding.md §P1 (NIST constraint clearly stated); src/agents/p3_regulatory_convergence/agent.py (constraint mentioned but not operationalized); src/agents/p4_risk_blindspot/agent.py (constraint not mentioned)
findings.problem: The NIST cluster constraint ('NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 are one authorship cluster and must not be treated as independent evidence') is explicitly documented for P1 in Methods Scaffolding: 'NIST cluster independence constraint: NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 mappings are treated as ONE evidence source.' The same constraint is stated for P3: 'within-NIST-cluster convergence is not treated as independent evidence of cross-framework convergence.' But P2 and P4 agent code do not mention the constraint at all. For P2 (graph topology), if the STRM mappings include edges from all three NIST frameworks and the graph is not split by framework origin, community detection may find a 'NIST cluster community' (all NIST controls clustering together) and report it as evidence of independent convergence — which violates the constraint. For P4 (coverage), if a risk category is well-covered by all three NIST frameworks, the agent might count it as 'triple coverage' when it should be counted as 'NIST-cluster coverage' (one evidence source). The constraint is clear for P1 but operationalization is delegated to individual agents without specification.
findings.evidence: Methods Scaffolding §P1: 'NIST cluster independence constraint: NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 mappings are treated as ONE evidence source... This constraint is enforced at the data layer, not at the analysis layer — it is not a choice made per analysis, it is a standing rule.' But p3_regulatory_convergence/agent.py specifies: 'NIST cluster constraint (MANDATORY): NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 are ONE authorship cluster' with no implementation details. The P2 and P4 agent stubs have no mention of the constraint. Data layer enforcement would mean filtering the split data to remove NIST intra-cluster edges before loading — but data_split.py makes no such distinction (it splits on control IDs, not mapping origin).
findings.recommendation: Explicitly operationalize the constraint: (1) In data_split.py or the DataStewardAgent, add an optional filter that tags each STRM mapping with its origin framework (NIST vs. non-NIST). (2) In each agent's code (P2–P5), document the constraint and implement it: P2 should tag community assignments with framework origin and report cross-framework vs. NIST-only clusters separately; P3 should exclude NIST intra-cluster dyads from convergence statistics; P4 should weight NIST-framework coverage as 0.33x (one framework, three implementations) not 3.0x; P5 should handle NIST control clustering separately. (3) Include a pre-execution assertion in each module: 'NIST cluster constraint applied: [COUNT] NIST intra-cluster findings flagged and excluded.' If the operationalization is deferred to individual agents without a shared implementation, the constraint will be inconsistently applied.

findings.title: LangGraph Architecture Supports Exploratory/Confirmatory Firewall but Not Methodology Validation
findings.problem: The LangGraph architecture correctly implements the structural firewall: test split is encrypted, Gate 2 releases the key, confirmatory analysis runs on test-only data. This is sound from a data-access-control perspective. However, the architecture does not validate that the methodology is sound for the questions being asked. The gate nodes check that hypotheses are registered (state.hypothesis_set is non-empty) but do not validate that hypotheses are appropriate for the split strategy. An agent could register a hypothesis like 'Community structure of the control graph differs from SCF domains' (which is invalid for test-only analysis) and the gate would pass it. The structural firewall prevents running confirmatory analysis on train+val data, but it does not prevent running invalid confirmatory analysis on test data. This is a gap between 'structural enforcement of data access' and 'methodological enforcement of valid analysis.'
findings.evidence: src/graph.py: gate_2 routes to either 'confirmatory_entry' or back to 'exploratory_phase' based on a gate decision, but the decision is recorded in state.gate_status[gate_2] with fields: 'gate_id', 'decision', 'rationale', 'reviewer'. The 'rationale' field is a string (reviewed by a human), not a structured checklist of methodological validations. src/nodes/gates.py: gate_2_node has no documented pre-check of hypothesis compatibility. The gate is a 'human approval gate' (ADR-0004 §2) but relies on the human reviewer to check methodology — structural validation is not in place.
findings.recommendation: Add a pre-Gate-2 validation function (gate_2_validation.py) that: (1) For each registered hypothesis, identifies the analysis type (correlation/classification vs. graph property vs. coverage); (2) Checks the split strategy compatibility; (3) Flags graph/coverage hypotheses as requiring exemption or redefinition; (4) Reports a structured validation report to the gate coordinator. The human reviewer can then make an informed decision ('Approve SAP' vs. 'Reject SAP and require revision'). This does not reduce human judgment; it surfaces the methodological issues for the human to decide on.

findings.title: Tier 2 Knowledge Graph Composition Assumes P2 Topology is Composable but Split Strategy May Prevent This
findings.problem: ADR-0010 specifies: 'P2 topology graphs must be designed for multi-graph composition. Tier 2 will ingest multiple framework graphs and run cross-graph algorithms.' The PROJECT_CHARTER requires: 'P2 topology graphs must be designed for multi-graph composition.' But if P2 confirmatory analysis is conducted on 15% of controls (test split), the P2 knowledge graph will represent only 15% of the SCF control space. When Tier 2 tries to compose multiple such subgraph outputs (P2 from SCF alone, P2 from NIST 800-53, P2 from CIS v8), it will be composing incomplete, non-representative topology graphs. Tier 2's cross-framework 'blast radius modeling' (tracing failures through the control graph) will lose edges that exist in the full corpus, producing underestimates of propagation. The program goal is 'knowledge graphs of each framework' and 'blast radius modeling'; if the graphs are subsampled, the modeling is invalid. This is a direct threat to the Tier 2 research objective.
findings.evidence: ADR-0010: 'Graph data models must compose... Tier 2 will ingest multiple framework graphs and run cross-graph algorithms... information cascade modeling on the combined graph.' PROJECT_CHARTER §1.5: 'P2 topology graphs must be designed for multi-graph composition.' But if P2 uses a 70/15/15 split and only confirmatory results (test set) are exported as knowledge graphs, those graphs have 15% of the nodes. Tier 2 cannot perform 'blast radius modeling' on an incomplete graph. Conversely, if all P2 exploratory results (85% of controls) are exported, they are not confirmatory and Tier 2 is building impact models on exploratory findings.
findings.recommendation: At Gate 2, decide: (A) Export P2 knowledge graphs from the full train+val corpus (85%), label all Tier 2 inputs as exploratory, and conduct Tier 2 analysis with that epistemic status; (B) Redesign P2 to produce confirmatory results on the full corpus and label all P2 findings as confirmatory; (C) Restructure Tier 1 so that P2 is a separate 'topology project' with its own full-corpus data access, independent of P1/P3/P4/P5 module sequencing. Option A is the most practical given current architecture. Option B requires the methodological fix noted in the P2 finding (redefine hypotheses for subgraph analysis or exempt from split). Option C requires an architectural change (separate graphs for topology vs. mapping analysis).

Perfect. I've completed the analysis. Let me provide a summary message:

## Analysis Complete: Critical Methodological Flaws in Train/Val/Test Split Firewall

I've conducted a thorough review of the HC-GRC research platform's architecture, methodology, and implementation against the stated research goal. The findings are sobering: **the exploratory/confirmatory firewall, while structurally sound for P1 (NLP/STRM correlation analysis), is fundamentally incompatible with P2, P3, P4, and P5 analysis designs.**

### Core Problem

The platform correctly implements a 70/15/15 train/val/test split with a structural data-access firewall (test split encrypted until Gate 2). This is appropriate for hypothesis-testing analyses where you're predicting individual instances (P1: NLP similarity scores predicting STRM labels). However, four of the five analytical modules (P2–P5) are designed to answer questions about **whole-system properties**:

- **P2**: "Do the 33 editorial domains correspond to natural communities in the **control graph**?" — Graph topology is destroyed by splitting nodes.
- **P3/P4**: "Which domains account for >60% of mappings?" and "Are the 39 risk categories uniformly covered?" — Coverage distributions differ between 85% and 15% subsamples.
- **P5**: "Which controls fall outside AAT when identified via NLP clustering?" — Cluster membership depends on the full embedding space; splitting controls changes cluster boundaries.

### Critical Findings (Severity: Critical or High)

1. **P2 Graph Topology Confirmatory Claims Are Invalid** — Community detection on a 15% subgraph produces incompatible results to detection on the full graph. Modularity Q, centrality scores, and community assignments all change.

2. **P3/P4 Coverage Hypotheses Are Untestable on Test Splits** — Risk coverage percentages and domain-mapping distributions are corpus properties, not generalizable statistics. A risk category with 30% coverage in the full corpus may have 28% or 35% in a random subsample.

3. **P5 Clustering Boundary Instability** — HDBSCAN cluster membership depends on the full dataset density. Removing 15% of controls can flip cluster assignments for the remaining controls.

4. **Exploratory Effect Sizes Leak Into Confirmatory Calibration** — The design acknowledges effect size inflation for data-adaptive hypotheses but relies on reporting transparency ("epistemic status labeling"), not structural prevention. Hypotheses with stronger exploratory effects are more likely to be registered.

5. **Unit of Analysis Underspecified** — The SAP declares "control" as the unit but P3/P4 analyses naturally operate at the mapping level (280,000 rows, not 1,400 controls). This risks entity-level leakage if not carefully enforced.

6. **Gate 2 Does Not Validate Methodological Consistency** — The gate checks that hypotheses are registered but not whether they're compatible with the split strategy. Invalid hypotheses can pass through.

7. **Tier 2 Downstream Goal Threatened** — ADR-0010 requires "P2 topology graphs designed for multi-graph composition," but subgraph outputs (15% of controls) are insufficient for Tier 2's "blast radius modeling."

### The Design's Strength and Limitation

The **structural firewall is correctly implemented**: test split is encrypted until Gate 2, preventing data leakage at the implementation level. This is good engineering.

The **fundamental tension is methodological, not architectural**: the split strategy assumes all analyses are instance-level predictions generalizable from samples. But graph structure, coverage distributions, and clustering boundaries are sample-sensitive and not generalizable from 15% subsets. No amount of clever engineering can fix this.

### Practical Path Forward

At Gate 2, the platform must choose one of three strategies:

**Option A (Pragmatic)**: Conduct P2–P5 on the full train+val corpus (85%) and label all outputs as exploratory. Export P2 knowledge graphs as "exploratory findings" to Tier 2. Document the trade-off: confirmatory rigor for P1, exploratory characterization for P2–P5.

**Option B (Methodologically Strict)**: Redefine P2–P5 hypotheses to be testable on subgraphs (e.g., "rank-order of domain centrality is preserved across splits" instead of "absolute community structure differs from domains"). This requires substantial rewriting of the charter.

**Option C (Architectural)**: Separate P2 into its own project with full-corpus data access, independent of the P1/P3/P4/P5 split. P2 becomes a characterization study (exploratory) published separately. P1/P3/P4/P5 use the split.

All three require explicit decisions at Gate 2 — the current state of accepting all charter hypotheses unchanged into confirmatory analysis is not viable.
